summary: Joel Spolsky announces the Block Protocol, an open standard for making web content blocks interchangeable across different editors, blogging tools, and content management systems. He observes that while many web editors have converged on the concept of insertable blocks (paragraphs, tables, kanban boards, etc.), each platform implements them from scratch with no interoperability. The Block Protocol aims to let any block work in any embedding application, so developers can write a block once and have it usable everywhere. He emphasizes the protocol is open, free, and non-proprietary, and highlights that blocks can carry typed, structured data making web content machine-readable without scraping.
key_points: Web editors have converged on the block metaphor (even standardizing on the / key) but every implementation is proprietary and incompatible
The Block Protocol is an open, free standard that lets any block be embedded in any conforming application, eliminating redundant reimplementation
Blocks can carry typed schemas, making structured data on the web machine-readable without screen scraping or manual data exports
The project is being released very early (v0.1) to attract community feedback and contributions before the protocol matures
money_quotes: We have seem to have standardized on one thing: the / key to insert a new block. Everything else, though, is completely proprietary and non-standard.
Until now, every app that wants blocks has to implement them from scratch. Want a calendar block? Some kind of fancy Kanban board? Something to embed image galleries? Code it up yourself, buddy.
As a result of the non-standardization of blocks, our end-users suffer.
I thought, wouldn't it be cool if blocks were interchangeable and reusable across the web?
Have you ever seen one of those websites where there's a link to 'download the data set in .XLS format'? Yeah, say goodbye to that.
Want to create a block that shows the Great Circle routing for a flight between two airports? Write the code for the block once and it can be embedded anywhere.
key_insight: Web editors have all converged on blocks as a UI paradigm but with zero interoperability, and an open protocol could eliminate redundant work while making structured web content machine-readable by default.
